meta.scene_summary: We are currently speedrunning the apocalypse while the infrastructure is still running on a expired SSL certificate.

cooking.headline: Meta is speedrunning the 'DIY Nuclear Power Plant' achievement
cooking.summary: Meta is buying eight nuclear reactors just to keep their AI training clusters running without a power grid meltdown. It's the ultimate flex for a company that just really wants to make sure their GPUs never have to take a nap.
cooking.plain_english: Meta has signed a contract to purchase electricity from eight upcoming nuclear power plants to support its massive data center energy requirements.

cooking.headline: Governments are trying to set a bedtime for the internet
cooking.summary: Countries are racing to restrict social media access for kids, effectively trying to ground an entire generation from their digital playgrounds. It's like telling a kid to stay off the grass, except the grass is an infinite scroll of dopamine and the fence is made of legislation.
cooking.plain_english: Several countries are implementing new laws to restrict or ban children from using social media platforms.

cooking.headline: GLM-5.2 is basically a supercomputer that lives in your nightmares
cooking.summary: GLM-5.2 is the most capable open-weight model yet, provided you have a spare data center to actually run it. Trying to host this locally is like buying a Ferrari and realizing you have to build your own private highway to drive it to the grocery store.
cooking.plain_english: A highly capable artificial intelligence model has been released for public use, but it requires extremely powerful computer hardware that is difficult for most individuals to access.

cooking.headline: Project Valhalla has been in the works longer than most startups
cooking.summary: After a decade of development, Java is finally getting value types to make memory management less of a headache. It's the ultimate 'I'll get to it eventually' project that actually finished, proving that sometimes even the slowest group project partners do show up on the final day.
cooking.plain_english: The Java programming language is introducing a new feature called Project Valhalla that improves how the system handles data to increase performance and efficiency.

cooking.headline: NASA just gave a billionaire a Mars contract, as if we're playing real-life SimCity now.
cooking.summary: NASA has tapped Eric Schmidt's rocket venture to compete with SpaceX for Mars missions, proving that space exploration is just a high-stakes group project where the richest kids get to choose the equipment. It's comforting to know that even on another planet, the same corporate rivalries from Silicon Valley will be there to greet us.
cooking.plain_english: NASA has selected Eric Schmidt's aerospace company to participate in missions to Mars, creating a new competitor to SpaceX for government space contracts.

cooked.headline: EU prepares to tell AWS and Azure that being the internet's landlord is a full-time job
cooked.summary: The EU is planning to classify major cloud providers as gatekeepers, which is basically the regulatory equivalent of being put on academic probation. It turns out that when you host the entire internet, the government eventually wants to sit on the board of directors.
cooked.plain_english: European regulators are moving to label Amazon Web Services and Microsoft Azure as gatekeepers, subjecting them to stricter competition rules under the Digital Services Act.

cooked.headline: Apple's A12 and A13 chips just found their permanent guest pass for hackers.
cooked.summary: Apple silicon is basically the fortress that forgot to lock the back door, and there is no firmware update coming to fix it. It is the hardware equivalent of realizing your 'unbreakable' bike lock was actually made of chocolate.
cooked.plain_english: A permanent security vulnerability has been discovered in Apple's A12 and A13 processors that cannot be fixed with a software update.

cooked.headline: Let's Encrypt had a minor existential crisis with SSL renewals today.
cooked.summary: The service that automates the internet's security decided to take a sick day, leaving everyone scrambling to renew their certificates manually. It is like the one guy who handles the keys to the office deciding to go on an unannounced vacation.
cooked.plain_english: Let's Encrypt experienced a technical issue that caused failures in the automated renewal process for digital security certificates.

cooked.headline: Amazon's AI strategy is apparently 'agree or you're fired'
cooked.summary: Amazon is investigating engineers who dared to question the environmental impact of their AI data centers. It turns out the only thing Amazon hates more than unionization is a developer who accidentally performs a code review on the company's PR department.
cooked.plain_english: Amazon is investigating employees who publicly criticized the company's plans to expand data centers for artificial intelligence.

cooked.headline: Turns out 'I didn't read the terms' isn't a valid legal defense for the company either.
cooked.summary: Elkjop ignored a warning that their forced consent practices were illegal, and five years later, they've paid €1.8 million to learn that the user isn't the only one who skips the fine print. It's the ultimate lesson in technical debt: the interest on ignoring compliance is significantly higher than just fixing the bug.
cooked.plain_english: The retailer Elkjop was fined 1.8 million euros for using illegal forced consent methods to collect customer data.
